items.title: Always work in log-likelihood for computation
items.avoid: Multiplying hundreds of PMF/PDF values directly and comparing tiny L(θ) numbers.
items.prefer: Sum log f(x_i | θ), use `logpmf`/`logpdf`, and minimize negative log-likelihood in optimizers.
items.why: Log is monotonic (same argmax) and avoids floating-point underflow on large datasets.
items.example: PyTorch `CrossEntropyLoss` and `BCEWithLogitsLoss` implement stable negative log-likelihood, not raw products.

items.title: State what is fixed before writing L(θ)
items.avoid: Jumping to formulas without naming observed data and free parameters.
items.prefer: Template: “Observed x = …; model f(x | θ); likelihood L(θ) = …”
items.why: The probability/likelihood distinction is purely about roles; explicit setup prevents swap errors.
items.example: “Observed: 7 heads in 10 flips → L(p) = p^7(1−p)^3” is unambiguous; “probability of p” is not.

items.title: Match likelihood factor to data type
items.avoid: Using Gaussian PDF factors on categorical labels or Bernoulli factors on unbounded counts.
items.prefer: Bernoulli/Categorical for classes, Poisson/Binomial for counts, Gaussian for continuous features—same as distribution choice.
items.why: Wrong f(x | θ) yields a well-defined but wrong L(θ), often showing up as miscalibrated loss and poor fit.
items.example: Daily ticket counts y ∈ {0,1,2,…} → Poisson logpmf(y | λ), not Normal pdf(y | μ, σ).

items.title: Check independence before product likelihood
items.avoid: Blindly summing per-row log-likelihood when rows are duplicated or correlated.
items.prefer: Document IID assumption; use grouped, hierarchical, or sequence models when dependence matters.
items.why: Product likelihood is exact only under the assumed joint model; violated independence misstates evidence.
items.example: Repeated measurements on the same patient should not enter L(θ) as independent Gaussian draws without a random-effects term.
